microscopic_findings.text: Cyst is lined by a delicate **single-layer flattened to low-cuboidal lining** with bland nuclei, consistent with **mesothelial lining**.
The wall shows dense **collagenous fibrous tissue** with admixed **mature adipose tissue** and small vessels.
Lumen contains **proteinaceous eosinophilic debris**, scattered **red blood cells**, and mild chronic inflammatory cells without granulomas.
There is no **ciliated respiratory epithelium**, **cartilage/bronchial glands**, or **thymic tissue/Hassall corpuscles**.

interpretation: The specimen shows a **benign mesothelial-lined cyst** characterized by a **bland attenuated mesothelial lining** over a fibrofatty collagenous wall. Mild luminal hemorrhagic and proteinaceous contents are reactive and there is no cytologic atypia, papillary proliferation, or invasive growth to suggest neoplasia. The absence of **ciliated respiratory epithelium, cartilage, or bronchial glands** argues against bronchogenic cyst, and the lack of **thymic tissue or Hassall corpuscles** excludes thymic cyst.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells and containing fibrofatty tissue. The wall is composed of dense fibrous tissue with scattered chronic inflammation.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a respiratory epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Low-power survey left-central tissue to locate cyst wall and cellular foci

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened to cuboidal cells, with a wall composed of dense fibrous tissue and adipose tissue. The lumen contains amorphous material and cellular debris.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a respiratory epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Low-power survey of central tissue to locate cystic spaces/linings

tiles.description:
The image shows fragmented fibrofatty tissue with scattered lymphoid aggregates and blood vessels. The tissue is largely acellular with some areas of dense fibrous tissue.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The presence of fibrofatty tissue and lymphoid aggregates is consistent with the wall of a pericardial cyst. The clinical presentation of a mass adjacent to the pericardium and the gross description of a bilingular cyst further support this diagnosis.

Differential diagnoses could include a thymic cyst or a lymphangioma, but the fibrofatty nature of the tissue and the clinical context favor a pericardial cyst.
tiles.rationale: Global low-power survey of main tissue region for overall architecture and possible cyst wall.

tiles.description:
The image shows fragmented fibrofatty tissue with scattered lymphoid aggregates and a large vessel. The tissue is fragmented and lacks a distinct lining.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a thymic cyst or a bronchogenic cyst, which would typically show a distinct epithelial lining.
tiles.rationale: Low-power survey central tissue

tiles.description:
The image shows fibrofatty tissue with scattered lymphoid aggregates and prominent blood vessels. The tissue is fragmented and lacks a distinct epithelial lining.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The differential diagnosis includes a thymic cyst, which would typically show a cystic structure lined by epithelium and containing thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Low-power survey of right-central tissue for luminal edges

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells and containing fibrofatty tissue. The wall is composed of dense fibrous tissue with scattered lymphoid aggregates.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which would typically have a respiratory epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which would contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Survey left portion for any continuous lining surface or distinct tissue type.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by stratified squamous epithelium with underlying fibrofatty tissue. The cyst lumen contains amorphous material and cellular debris.

The most likely diagnosis is a bronchogenic cyst. Differential diagnoses include a pericardial cyst, which typically lacks a true epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Low-power survey right-central tissue

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells and containing fibrofatty tissue. The wall is composed of dense fibrous tissue with scattered adipocytes.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which would typically have a respiratory epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which would contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Low-power survey of lower-left tissue where folded wall may show lining

tiles.description:
The image shows fragmented fibrofatty tissue with scattered lymphoid aggregates and a prominent nerve bundle. The tissue is consistent with a benign cystic lesion.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a thymic cyst or a bronchogenic cyst, which can be distinguished by their specific epithelial lining and associated structures.
tiles.rationale: Survey right elongated portion for luminal edge/lining and wall thickness variation.

tiles.description:
The image shows fragmented fibrofatty tissue with scattered lymphoid aggregates. The tissue is composed of mature adipocytes and fibrous stroma.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The presence of fibrofatty tissue and lymphoid aggregates is consistent with the wall of a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include thymic cyst or lymphangioma, which would typically show different epithelial or endothelial lining features.
tiles.rationale: Low-power survey inferior tissue where thicker wall/fat may be present

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by stratified squamous epithelium with underlying fibrofatty tissue. The cyst wall contains adnexal structures including hair follicles and sebaceous glands.

The most likely diagnosis is a dermoid cyst. Differential diagnoses include epidermoid cyst, which lacks adnexal structures, and a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has respiratory epithelium and cartilage.
tiles.rationale: Low-power survey of lower-right tongue-like extension for surface lining

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by stratified squamous epithelium with underlying lymphoid tissue. The cyst wall contains fibrofatty tissue and smooth muscle.

The most likely diagnosis is a bronchogenic cyst. Differential diagnoses include a thymic cyst, which would typically show thymic tissue rather than lymphoid tissue, and a pericardial cyst, which is usually lined by mesothelium.
tiles.rationale: Survey inferior portion where thicker tissue may contain fat/lymphoid aggregates.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with underlying fibrofatty tissue. The lumen contains amorphous material and scattered cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which would typically have a ciliated epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which would show thymic tissue in the wall.
tiles.rationale: Zoom into cyst lining seen at 2.5x to characterize cell type

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with a fibrofatty wall. The lumen contains amorphous material, red blood cells, and scattered inflammatory cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a ciliated epithelial lining, and a lymphangioma, which would show lymphatic channels.
tiles.rationale: Interrogate reported stratified squamous lining; confirm epithelial type vs mesothelium/metaplasia

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells, containing fibrofatty tissue with adipocytes and fibrous septa, and a thick-walled blood vessel. The surrounding tissue is fibrofatty with scattered inflammatory cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a respiratory epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Assess purported cyst lining in left portion; confirm mesothelial vs epithelial at higher low-power.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with underlying fibrofatty tissue. The lumen contains amorphous material, cellular debris, and scattered inflammatory cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a ciliated epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Assess lymphoid aggregates noted at low power; look for germinal centers/thymic tissue

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened to cuboidal cells with a fibrous wall containing blood vessels and scattered lymphoid aggregates. The lumen is clear.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which would typically have a ciliated epithelial lining, and a lymphangioma, which would show lymphatic channels within the wall.
tiles.rationale: Assess area described as squamous lining/lymphoid; verify at 5x for architecture (skin vs cyst vs contamination).

tiles.description:
The image shows fibrofatty tissue with numerous blood vessels, some containing red blood cells, and scattered inflammatory cells. The tissue appears fragmented.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The presence of fibrofatty tissue and blood vessels is consistent with the wall of a cyst. The clinical presentation of a mass adjacent to the pericardium and the gross description of a bilingular cyst further support this diagnosis.

Differential diagnoses could include a thymic cyst or a bronchogenic cyst, but the fibrofatty nature of the tissue and the location adjacent to the pericardium are more characteristic of a pericardial cyst.
tiles.rationale: Second cystic area—confirm lining and look for mesothelial features

tiles.description:
The image shows fibrofatty tissue with scattered adipocytes and fibrous stroma, along with a large eosinophilic structure and a small vessel. The presence of fibrofatty tissue is consistent with a benign cystic lesion.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The differential diagnosis includes a bronchogenic cyst, which would typically show respiratory epithelium and cartilage, and a thymic cyst, which would show thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Further characterize lymphoid aggregates/inflammation in fibrofatty wall

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with underlying fibrofatty tissue. The lumen contains amorphous material and cellular debris.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a respiratory epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Assess right portion with nerve bundle and wall; look for lining surface and wall composition.

tiles.description:
The image shows fibrofatty tissue with mature adipocytes and fibrous stroma, along with areas of hemorrhage and inflammation. The presence of fibrofatty tissue in a cystic structure is consistent with a benign process.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The key features supporting this diagnosis are the presence of fibrofatty tissue and the clinical context of a cystic mass adjacent to the pericardium. Differential diagnoses could include a thymic cyst or a bronchogenic cyst, but the fibrofatty tissue is more characteristic of a pericardial cyst.
tiles.rationale: Area with lymphoid aggregates/vessels—look for epithelial rests or lining fragments

tiles.description:
The image shows mature adipose tissue with prominent, thickened fibrous septa containing scattered inflammatory cells and small blood vessels. The adipocytes are uniform with clear cytoplasm and peripherally located nuclei.

The most likely diagnosis is a fibrolipoma, a benign tumor composed of mature adipose tissue and fibrous tissue. A lipoma is a primary differential diagnosis, but the prominent fibrous component favors fibrolipoma. Other possibilities like liposarcoma are less likely given the uniform appearance of the adipocytes and lack of atypia.
tiles.rationale: Evaluate cyst wall/lining and chronic inflammation; look for mesothelial lining

tiles.description:
The image shows fragmented fibrofatty tissue with scattered lymphoid aggregates and a prominent nerve bundle. The tissue is largely acellular with some amorphous material.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The presence of fibrofatty tissue and lymphoid aggregates is consistent with the wall of a pericardial cyst. The clinical presentation of a mass adjacent to the pericardium and the gross description of a bilingular cyst further support this diagnosis.

Differential diagnoses include a thymic cyst or a lymphangioma. A thymic cyst would typically show thymic tissue, which is not evident here. A lymphangioma would show dilated lymphatic channels, which are not seen in this image.
tiles.rationale: Central region overview at 5x to look for continuous lining and inflammation/lymphoid aggregates.

tiles.description:
The image shows fibrofatty tissue with scattered spindle cells and abundant extravasated red blood cells. The spindle cells have elongated, wavy nuclei and are arranged in fascicles.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The presence of fibrofatty tissue and spindle cells is consistent with the wall of a cyst. The extravasated red blood cells may represent hemorrhage within the cyst.

Differential diagnoses include a fibrovascular polyp or a benign mesenchymal tumor such as a solitary fibrous tumor. However, the overall morphology, particularly the fibrofatty component and the clinical context of a cystic lesion adjacent to the pericardium, strongly favors a pericardial cyst.
tiles.rationale: Apparent squamous/adnexal area—verify if true skin contaminant vs cyst wall

tiles.description:
The image shows fragmented fibrofatty tissue with scattered dark granular material. The tissue is composed of mature adipocytes and fibrous stroma.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The presence of fibrofatty tissue is consistent with the wall of a pericardial cyst. The dark granular material may represent hemosiderin or other debris.

Differential diagnoses include a thymic cyst or a bronchogenic cyst. A thymic cyst would typically show thymic tissue, which is not evident here. A bronchogenic cyst would show respiratory epithelium, which is also absent.
tiles.rationale: Survey central darker focus for lymphoid aggregate/germinal center

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with underlying fibrofatty tissue. The lumen contains amorphous material and scattered cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which would typically have a ciliated epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which would show thymic tissue in the wall.
tiles.rationale: High power of best-demonstrated cyst lining to decide mesothelial vs epithelial

tiles.description:
The image shows fibrofatty tissue with mature adipocytes and fibrous stroma, along with numerous blood vessels and scattered inflammatory cells. The presence of fibrofatty tissue in a cystic structure is consistent with a pericardial cyst.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which would typically show respiratory epithelium, and a thymic cyst, which would show thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Detail of flattened lining on left cystic area—confirm mesothelial-like single layer vs true epithelium.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cyst wall composed of dense fibrous tissue with scattered spindle cells and chronic inflammation. The lumen contains amorphous material and cellular debris.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a respiratory epithelial lining, and a lymphangioma, which would show lymphatic channels.
tiles.rationale: High power of suspected lymphoid aggregate to assess for germinal center/thymic tissue

tiles.description:
The image shows a cyst wall composed of dense fibrous tissue with scattered spindle cells and chronic inflammation. The lumen contains amorphous material and cellular debris.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a respiratory epithelial lining, and a lymphangioma, which would show lymphatic channels.
tiles.rationale: Adjacent luminal edge—look for hobnailing/papillae/reactive atypia

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened to cuboidal cells with a wall composed of dense fibrous tissue and smooth muscle. The lumen contains amorphous material and cellular debris.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a ciliated epithelial lining, and a mesothelial cyst, which is lined by mesothelial cells.
tiles.rationale: Detail of lining and adjacent lymphoid aggregates; assess for germinal centers/inflammation.

tiles.description:
The image shows fibrofatty tissue with numerous blood vessels, some containing thrombi, and areas of hemorrhage. A large, thick-walled vessel is also present.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The presence of fibrofatty tissue and blood vessels is consistent with the wall of a cyst. The clinical presentation of a mass adjacent to the pericardium and the gross description of a bilingular cyst attached to the pericardium and diaphragmatic pleura support this diagnosis. The hemorrhage and thrombi are likely secondary changes within the cyst.

Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically contains respiratory epithelium and cartilage, and a lymphangioma, which would show lymphatic channels.
tiles.rationale: Check linear wall areas for lining and inflammation

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with underlying fibrofatty tissue. The lumen contains amorphous material, red blood cells, and scattered inflammatory cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a ciliated epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Another candidate luminal surface in left-central tissue

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with a wall composed of fibrous tissue and smooth muscle. The lumen contains amorphous material and scattered red blood cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a ciliated epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Detail of right-side lining with luminal debris; look for cilia/mucin, papillae, or reactive change.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with underlying fibrofatty tissue. The lumen contains amorphous material, cellular debris, and scattered inflammatory cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a ciliated epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Assess nerve bundle/fibrofatty wall area for any other tissue types (muscle/cartilage/thymic).

tiles.description:
The image shows fibrofatty tissue with scattered spindle cells and a prominent nerve bundle. There is also amorphous eosinophilic material and scattered red blood cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The presence of fibrofatty tissue and a nerve bundle is consistent with the wall of a pericardial cyst. The amorphous eosinophilic material and red blood cells may represent contents within the cyst.

Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically contains respiratory epithelium and cartilage, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: High power of lining in right-sided cystic area; confirm mesothelial vs squamous/respiratory

tiles.description:
The image shows a fragment of fibrofatty tissue with scattered spindle cells and mature adipocytes. There is also amorphous eosinophilic material and cellular debris.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The presence of fibrofatty tissue is consistent with the wall of a pericardial cyst. The clinical presentation of a discrete mass adjacent to the pericardium and the gross findings of a cystic structure attached to the pericardium and diaphragmatic pleura support this diagnosis. Differential diagnoses could include a thymic cyst or a bronchogenic cyst, but the location and gross description are more typical of a pericardial cyst.
tiles.rationale: Sample additional luminal edge in mid tissue to confirm lining consistency

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells and containing fibrofatty tissue with scattered inflammatory cells. The wall is composed of dense fibrous tissue.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a respiratory epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Search for any skin adnexa or stratified squamous epithelium to exclude epidermoid/dermoid inclusion.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by stratified squamous epithelium with underlying fibrofatty tissue. The lumen contains amorphous eosinophilic material and red blood cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a bronchogenic cyst. Differential diagnoses include a pericardial cyst, which typically lacks an epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may have a different epithelial lining and associated thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Right-side spindle/hemorrhagic area—exclude true squamous/skin contaminant and look for lining fragments

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells and containing fibrofatty tissue with scattered inflammatory cells and hemorrhage. The wall is composed of dense fibrous tissue.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a respiratory epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Survey superior-left bandlike tissue for lymphoid aggregates or epithelial-lined spaces

tiles.description:
The image shows a cyst wall composed of fibrous tissue with a prominent blood vessel and scattered adipose tissue. The lumen contains amorphous material and red blood cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which would typically show respiratory epithelium, and a thymic cyst, which would show thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Revisit right-sided purported squamous lining to verify if true epithelial lining vs artifact/contaminant; look for adnexal structures/cartilage

tiles.description:
The image shows mature adipose tissue with areas of fibrous stroma and a thick-walled blood vessel. The adipocytes are large and clear with peripherally located nuclei.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The presence of mature adipose tissue and fibrous stroma is consistent with the gross description of a cyst composed of fibrofatty tissue. The thick-walled blood vessel is also a common finding in pericardial cysts.

Differential diagnoses include a thymic cyst or a bronchogenic cyst. A thymic cyst would typically show thymic tissue, which is not seen in this image. A bronchogenic cyst would show respiratory epithelium, which is also absent.
tiles.rationale: Check upper-left thinner edge for best preserved continuous lining cells.

tiles.description:
The image shows fibrofatty tissue with mature adipocytes and fibrous stroma, along with scattered inflammatory cells and red blood cells. The presence of fibrofatty tissue in a cystic structure is consistent with a benign process.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The key features supporting this diagnosis are the presence of fibrofatty tissue within a cystic structure, which is typical for pericardial cysts. The clinical presentation of a discrete homogeneous mass partially overlying the left cardiac border and the gross description of a bilingular cyst attached to the pericardium and diaphragmatic pleura further support this diagnosis.

Differential diagnoses to consider include a thymic cyst or a bronchogenic cyst. However, thymic cysts typically contain thymic tissue, and bronchogenic cysts often have respiratory epithelium and smooth muscle, which are not observed in this image.
tiles.rationale: High power on any lymphoid aggregate/lining in superior-left area

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened to cuboidal epithelium, with a wall containing dense lymphoid tissue and fibrofatty tissue. The lumen contains amorphous material and red blood cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a ciliated pseudostratified columnar epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which would contain thymic tissue in the wall.
tiles.rationale: Assess darker basophilic nodule for lymphoid aggregate vs something else.

tiles.description:
The image shows fibrofatty tissue with scattered adipocytes and fibrous stroma. There is a prominent inflammatory infiltrate composed of lymphocytes and plasma cells, along with areas of hemorrhage and amorphous eosinophilic material.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The presence of fibrofatty tissue and chronic inflammation is consistent with the wall of a pericardial cyst. The clinical presentation of a mass adjacent to the pericardium and the gross findings of a bilingular cyst attached to the pericardium and diaphragmatic pleura further support this diagnosis.

Differential diagnoses to consider include a thymic cyst or a bronchogenic cyst. A thymic cyst would typically show thymic tissue, which is not evident in this image. A bronchogenic cyst would likely contain respiratory epithelium and cartilage, which are also absent.
tiles.rationale: Check far upper-right extension for surface lining; could represent mesothelial-lined cyst wall

tiles.description:
The image shows fibrofatty tissue with scattered lymphoid aggregates and a prominent blood vessel. The tissue is fragmented and appears to be part of a cyst wall.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The presence of fibrofatty tissue and lymphoid aggregates is consistent with the wall of a pericardial cyst. The clinical presentation of a mass adjacent to the pericardium and the gross description of a bilingular cyst further support this diagnosis.

Differential diagnoses to consider include a thymic cyst or a bronchogenic cyst. A thymic cyst would typically show thymic tissue, which is not evident in this image. A bronchogenic cyst would likely contain respiratory epithelium and cartilage, which are also not present.
tiles.rationale: Survey superior-central/right areas for possible lining and inflammatory foci

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with a wall composed of fibrous tissue and smooth muscle. The lumen contains amorphous material and cellular debris.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a ciliated epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Survey central area not yet emphasized for cartilage/glands/respiratory epithelium.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with underlying fibrofatty tissue. The lumen contains amorphous material, scattered cells, and numerous small, round, refractile structures.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The presence of a cystic structure lined by flattened cells and containing fibrofatty tissue is characteristic. The refractile structures in the lumen may represent cholesterol crystals, which can be seen in cysts.

Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a respiratory epithelial lining and may contain mucin, and a thymic cyst, which would show thymic tissue in the wall.
tiles.rationale: Sample extreme right tip tissue for any lining or respiratory epithelium

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells and containing fibrofatty tissue with scattered lymphoid aggregates. The cyst wall is composed of dense fibrous tissue.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a respiratory epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Examine far right tip for lining preservation and wall composition.

tiles.description:
The image shows fibrofatty tissue with numerous blood vessels, some containing red blood cells, and scattered inflammatory cells. The vessels exhibit thickened walls and narrowed lumens.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The presence of fibrofatty tissue and blood vessels is consistent with the wall of a cyst. The clinical presentation of a discrete mass adjacent to the pericardium and the gross findings of a bilingular cyst attached to the pericardium and diaphragmatic pleura support this diagnosis. Differential diagnoses could include a thymic cyst or a bronchogenic cyst, but the location and gross description are more characteristic of a pericardial cyst.
tiles.rationale: High power to check for mesothelium, granulomas, cholesterol clefts

tiles.description:
The image shows a cyst wall composed of fibrofatty tissue with scattered inflammatory cells and red blood cells. The cyst lumen contains amorphous material and cellular debris.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which would typically show respiratory epithelium, and a thymic cyst, which would show thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Sample upper-left thickened area for lining consistency and exclude skin/epidermoid component

tiles.description:
The image shows fragmented fibrofatty tissue with scattered lymphoid aggregates. The tissue is composed of mature adipocytes and fibrous stroma.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The presence of fibrofatty tissue and lymphoid aggregates is consistent with the wall of a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include thymic cyst or lymphangioma, which would typically show different epithelial or endothelial lining features.
tiles.rationale: Inferior-central thick tissue for smooth muscle or other specialized structures.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened to cuboidal cells with a wall composed of dense fibrous tissue and smooth muscle. The lumen contains amorphous material and cellular debris.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include bronchogenic cyst and thymic cyst, which can be distinguished by their characteristic epithelial lining and associated structures.
tiles.rationale: Assess inferior-left thicker fragment for lymphoid tissue/thymic rests or granulomas

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened to cuboidal cells, with a wall composed of dense fibrous tissue and smooth muscle. Adjacent lymphoid tissue is also present.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst.

Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which would typically have a ciliated epithelial lining, and a mesothelial cyst, which would be lined by mesothelial cells.
tiles.rationale: Focus on lymphoid-rich area to characterize as reactive aggregates vs thymic tissue.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened to cuboidal epithelium, surrounded by dense fibrous tissue with chronic inflammation and congested blood vessels. The lumen contains amorphous material and red blood cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which would typically have a ciliated columnar epithelial lining, and a lymphangioma, which would show lymphatic channels.
tiles.rationale: High power in same area for germinal centers, cholesterol clefts, granulomas

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with a fibrofatty wall. The lumen contains amorphous material, cellular debris, and scattered erythrocytes.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a ciliated epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Assess reported smooth muscle in wall; verify and look for specialized structures.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with a wall composed of fibrofatty tissue. The lumen contains amorphous material and cellular debris.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a respiratory epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Far inferior-left edge fragment; check for epithelial lining/skin adnexa or thymic tissue

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with a wall composed of fibrofatty tissue containing scattered spindle cells and small vessels. The lumen contains amorphous material and cellular debris.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a ciliated epithelial lining, and a lymphangioma, which would show endothelial-lined channels.
tiles.rationale: Evaluate far right lining at higher power for mesothelial features vs epithelium.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with underlying fibrofatty tissue. The lumen contains amorphous material, scattered red blood cells, and inflammatory cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a ciliated epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: High power to evaluate any lymphoid aggregate or lining at edge

tiles.description:
The image shows a cyst wall composed of dense fibrous tissue with scattered spindle cells and a prominent blood vessel. The lumen contains amorphous eosinophilic material and cellular debris.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically contains respiratory epithelium, and a lymphangioma, which would show lymphatic channels.
tiles.rationale: High power of lining where present to best characterize cell type (mesothelial vs epithelial) and exclude atypia.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by a single layer of flattened to cuboidal cells with round to oval nuclei, supported by a fibrous wall. The lumen is clear.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which would typically have a ciliated epithelial lining, and a mesothelial cyst, which would have a mesothelial lining.
tiles.rationale: High power of lymphoid aggregate adjacent lining to exclude thymic Hassall corpuscles or epithelial nests.

tiles.description:
The image shows fibrofatty tissue with prominent blood vessels and scattered inflammatory cells. The vessels exhibit thickened walls and narrowed lumens.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The presence of fibrofatty tissue and prominent vessels is consistent with the gross description of a bilingular cyst. The thickened vessel walls may be related to the patient's history of hypertension.
tiles.rationale: Focus on area with lymphoid aggregates at 5x (central-right); assess for germinal centers/thymic tissue

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened to cuboidal cells with a fibrofatty wall. The lumen contains amorphous material and red blood cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a ciliated epithelial lining, and a lymphangioma, which would show lymphatic channels.
tiles.rationale: Nearby cellular focus; exclude granuloma/cholesterol clefts/hemosiderin

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with abundant eosinophilic material and scattered red blood cells within the lumen. The wall is composed of fibrofatty tissue.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a ciliated epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Area with hemorrhage/thrombi at 5x; confirm hemorrhage/organization and inflammation

tiles.description:
The image shows a cyst wall composed of dense fibrous tissue with scattered spindle cells and numerous small blood vessels. The lumen contains amorphous material and scattered red blood cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically contains respiratory epithelium, and a lymphangioma, which would show lymphatic channels.
tiles.rationale: Dark granular material at 5x; evaluate for hemosiderin/cholesterol clefts

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened to cuboidal cells with a wavy contour, surrounded by dense fibrous tissue. The lumen contains amorphous material and scattered cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which would typically have a ciliated epithelial lining, and a mesothelial cyst, which would have a mesothelial lining.
tiles.rationale: Best candidate lining described as flattened to cuboidal with smooth muscle; assess mesothelial vs epithelial metaplasia and inflammation

tiles.description:
The image shows a paucicellular specimen with scattered spindle cells and abundant amorphous eosinophilic material. Scattered red blood cells are also present.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. The presence of fibrofatty tissue and the clinical context of a cystic mass adjacent to the pericardium support this diagnosis. Differential diagnoses include a thymic cyst or a cystic mesothelioma, which would typically show different cellular components or lining.
tiles.rationale: Right-sided cystic area; confirm if any true stratified squamous/respiratory epithelium present

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with underlying fibrofatty tissue. The lumen contains amorphous material, red blood cells, and scattered inflammatory cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which typically has a ciliated epithelial lining, and a thymic cyst, which may contain thymic tissue.
tiles.rationale: Evaluate eosinophilic structure mentioned at 10x for Hassall corpuscle vs hyalinized collagen/vessel

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with underlying fibrofatty tissue. Scattered inflammatory cells and red blood cells are present in the background.

The most likely diagnosis is a pericardial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which would typically show respiratory epithelium, and a lymphangioma, which would have lymphatic channels.
tiles.rationale: Check lining at central cystic area; look for cilia, goblet cells, basement membrane, mesothelial features
